retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Differential Diagnosis -- Amelogenesis Imperfecta
retrieved_docs.content: Dental fluorosis Chronological enamel hypoplasia Molar-Incisal Hypomineralization (MIH)

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Clinical Significance -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Molar-incisor hypomineralization (MIH) is the hypomineralization of systemic origin of one to four permanent first molars, frequently associated with affected incisors. It is a qualitative defect of the enamel that happens when ameloblasts are impaired in late amelogenesis stages: mineralization or maturation. It presents as white, yellow, or brown demarcated enamel opacities located, mainly on the vestibular face of the teeth (in the middle of the incisal third), with a clear distinction between the affected and sound enamel. [17] Although the etiology of the lesions is not defined, they have been related to birth prematurity, digestive system disease, asthma, frequent high fever, renal failure, and dioxins.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of amelogenesis imperfecta depends widely on the gene mutation of the population studied. Different studies have found that the incidence of amelogenesis imperfecta ranges from 1 in 700 to 1 in 14,000. [11] [6] Dentinogenesis imperfecta has an incidence ranging from 1 in 6,000 to 1 in 8,000 cases throughout the United States, with no gender predilection. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the deciduous dentition, hypodontia appears in <1% of individuals of White racial background, whereas Japanese populations demonstrate a significantly higher prevalence. [9] In contrast, the permanent dentition displays a broader prevalence range, with incidence reported between 1.6% and 36.5%, depending on the studied population. [7] Gender-related trends show no predilection for hypodontia in the primary dentition; however, a slight female predominance appears in permanent dentition cases. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Taurodontism occurs more frequently in male individuals and has been associated with X-linked syndromes. [14] The incidence of this condition is reportedly less than 1%, with a higher prevalence in Arctic and Native American populations. [15] Fusion has been observed in 0.21% of a population of 1,200 patients and has no known sex predilection. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypodontia and oligodontia often fall under the broader classification of “hypodontia,” with specific distinctions made based on the number of missing teeth. Hypodontia represents the most common dentofacial malformation in humans. [7] A clear correlation exists between hypodontia in the deciduous (primary) dentition and the corresponding permanent dentition; patients missing a primary tooth frequently lack its permanent successor. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Hypominerazlied amelogenesis imperfecta, or type III, is the most severe form of amelogenesis imperfecta due to the reduced enamel mineral content. Teeth with this condition are very sensitive to temperature and tactile pressure, presenting clinically with dark yellow or brown enamel color. On radiographic examination, enamel and dentin may appear to have the same radiodensity. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dens invaginatus has no gender predilection and a prevalence between 0.3 to 10%, depending on the population studied. [9] This condition is mainly identified on the maxillary lateral incisors, followed by the maxillary central incisors. It is very rarely discovered in any other teeth. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses of dental fluorosis are extensive. They include and are not limited to early carious lesions, molar-incisor hypomineralisation, developmental disorders of enamel and dentine, including amelogenesis imperfecta, Turner hypoplasia, tetracycline staining, and dental manifestations of celiac disease. [29] [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Amelogenesis imperfecta, as well as dentinogenesis imperfecta, has a good prognosis with appropriate management. In cases where patients are asymptomatic or do not express an esthetic concern, most patients will have a good quality of life with no or little treatment. More involved cases may require more invasive or multi-disciplinary treatment, which can potentially change the prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Differential Diagnosis -- Taurodontism
retrieved_docs.content: Amelogenesis imperfecta Dentinogenesis imperfecta X-chromosomal Aneuploidy

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Clinical Significance -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: White spot is a popular term to name a non-cavitated or initial caries lesion. However, it refers to the lesion's color and has no bearing on its activity. That is why it may be confused with other types of dental white lesions, such as dental fluorosis or molar incisor hypomineralization (MIH). [2] The principal differences between these pathologies are their etiology and location. As mentioned previously in this article, the etiology of the initial lesions is dental caries. They are commonly located on plaque accumulation areas: the gingival or cervical margins and the pits and fissures of the molars.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Differential Diagnosis -- Dentinogenesis Imperfecta
retrieved_docs.content: Dentin dysplasia Osteogenesis imperfecta Amelogenesis imperfecta

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The various clinical presentations of amelogenesis imperfecta depend on the gene that is mutated. [15] Hypoplastic amelogenesis imperfecta, or type I, presents with localized or generalized thinning of the enamel, resulting in the crown of the teeth appearing yellow or light brown in color. The surfaces of these teeth are often pitted or mottled in appearance. There is no pain associated with this subtype; however, thermal sensitivity has been reported. [20] Hypomature amelogenesis imperfecta, or type II, has a defect in matrix protein degradation. Enamel in this condition appears whitish to brown in color with no noted translucency, which commonly presents with enamel breakdown. Radiographic examination reveals a decreased enamel opacity, especially along the dentino-enamel junction. Type II is the mildest form, with the main concern being esthetics. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dens evaginatus/talon cusps are relatively common, with a prevalence ranging between 1 and 6%, and are mostly seen in the permanent dentition.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Hypodontia, regardless of severity, often becomes apparent early in life. The condition may first be recognized during the eruption of the deciduous dentition when a tooth fails to appear. In many cases, however, diagnosis occurs later as the permanent dentition begins to emerge, and 1 or more teeth do not erupt as expected. Clinical examination provides an initial assessment, while panoramic radiographs serve as essential diagnostic tools for confirming the presence or absence of teeth. [19]

retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811

This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.content: trauma, surgery, age‑related degeneration, calcific deposition, congenital malformations, and metastatic cancer each generate distinct but often overlapping structural and functional disturbances in the posterior segment, necessitating comprehensive ocular assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is categorized as a specific type of maternal condition that can complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, and it is considered a subcategory under the broader classification of other conditions or status of the mother complicating these reproductive processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 6 -- Community 2932
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is recognized as an etiological descriptor that can be applied to several clinical entities. It may be used to indicate the cause of ولادة قبل الأوان (premature birth), تمزق (rupture or tear), and حَصاة (stone formation). Recognizing these etiological links helps clinicians accurately document and address the underlying causes of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.
